title: Callaloo
subtitle: Jamaican-style leafy greens sautéed with onion, garlic, thyme, and scotch bonnet.
excerpt: Tinned callaloo or fresh spring greens sautéed with onion, tomato, thyme, and a whole scotch bonnet. The Saturday-morning vegetable side that goes with everything from ackee to brown stew. Twenty minutes, four ingredients.
sourceNotes: Callaloo is the Jamaican name for the leafy green dish made traditionally from the leaves of the taro plant (also called dasheen leaves). Outside Jamaica, the leaves of the amaranth or Chinese spinach plant are commonly used. The dish travels across the English-speaking Caribbean with regional variation: Trinidadian callaloo is a soup with okra and coconut milk; Jamaican callaloo is a sautéed side. Outside the Caribbean, tinned Jamaican-style callaloo or fresh spring greens substitute well.

content.text: Callaloo is the everyday Caribbean leafy green: a quick sauté with onion, garlic, tomato, thyme, and the whole scotch bonnet for warmth without proper heat. It accompanies almost any Jamaican dish, from ackee and saltfish at breakfast to brown stew chicken at dinner. It is also a complete, satisfying lunch on its own with a piece of fried plantain and a slice of hard-dough bread.

Tinned callaloo is the easiest source outside the Caribbean and is what most Jamaican households in the UK actually use. Fresh spring greens or cavolo nero work nearly as well and are sometimes easier to find; treat them the same way. Spinach is too soft; it collapses to nothing in this preparation.

items.name: Spring greens
items.prepNote: tough stalks removed, shredded; or a 540 g tin of callaloo, drained

content.text: A wide frying pan or a large saucepan with a lid. The greens need room to wilt before they steam down.

Warm {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide pan over a medium heat. Add the chopped {{onion}} onion and cook for 4 minutes until softened. Stir in {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{spring-onion}} sliced spring onions, the chopped {{tomato}} tomato, and {{thyme}} of thyme. Cook for 3 minutes more until the tomato breaks down.

Add the shredded {{spring-greens}} of greens (or drained tinned callaloo), the whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Pour in {{water}} of water. Toss to coat the greens in the oil and aromatics.

Cover the pan. Cook for 6 minutes for tinned callaloo, 10 minutes for fresh greens. Lift the lid once to stir at the halfway point. The greens should be just tender, still bright in colour, not collapsed. Lift out the whole scotch bonnet before serving (do not eat it whole). Taste and adjust the salt.

items.fix: Stop cooking the moment the greens are tender. Eight minutes is usually enough for fresh greens; tinned only needs 5 to 6 minutes.
items.cause: Overcooked, or covered with an acid lid (tomato juice spread across the top).

items.fix: Drain off any excess water at the end and reduce uncovered for 2 minutes to concentrate the flavour.
items.cause: Tinned callaloo not drained, or fresh greens released too much water under the lid.

items.fix: Add another half teaspoon of salt and a splash of soy sauce. The scotch bonnet should sit whole in the pan throughout, releasing flavour without breaking apart.
items.cause: Under-seasoned, or scotch bonnet too small to perfume the dish.

items.fix: Serve with rice and avoid the bowl with the broken pepper. Next time, choose a firm, unbruised scotch bonnet and place it on top of the greens, not buried underneath.
items.cause: The skin punctured during cooking, releasing the heat into the dish.

content.text: For a one-pot meal, add a tin of drained black beans or chickpeas with the greens and serve over rice. For a Trinidadian-leaning version, add a tin of coconut milk and 200 g of sliced okra; reduce uncovered for 5 minutes to thicken. A scrambled egg on top turns it into a Caribbean shakshuka of sorts.

Callaloo is the default green vegetable across Jamaican households. The dish is so foundational that the word callaloo refers both to the leaves themselves (the taro or amaranth greens) and to the cooked dish. In the UK, Brixton, Tooting, and Birmingham's Soho Road carry tinned callaloo on every Caribbean food shop shelf; outside the cities, spring greens are the everyday substitute.
